decisions.support_rationale: Evidence shows labeling by impact/sensitivity helped specific cases (outages, deadlines) reach responders or avoid being hidden, but does not support the universal causal claim that it 'ensures' all high-impact issues are addressed promptly.

The counterexample experience explicitly documents a widespread login failure waiting behind low-impact VIP requests due to ranking solely by requester status, directly supporting the claim that critical issues can be overlooked.

Evidence observes that outages reached responders quickly under this labeling scheme, but does not support the absolute predictive claim that high-impact issues 'will be addressed before' all lower-impact ones in every instance.
No evidence in the bundle describes a 'combined score' mechanism or confirms that resolution order strictly reflects such a calculated metric; evidence only notes labeling and general outcomes.
The specific quantitative success criterion of 'At least 90%' is entirely absent from the qualitative observational evidence provided in the bundle.

The supplied counterexample (px_02_counter_3) exactly matches the falsification condition described: a critical outage delayed behind low-impact VIP requests, validating the logical structure of the falsification statement.
The evidence explicitly states that a widespread login failure (critical outage) waited behind low-impact VIP requests, directly confirming the observable event described in the claim.
